_purpose: Seasonal calendar for the LA Acadiana cluster. Month-by-month rhythms across two eras: the agricultural era (1760-1900, combining sugar cane, cattle, and subsistence farming) and the mixed economy era (1900-1960, sugar, rice, oil, and fishing). Acadiana's calendar was shaped by the subtropical climate -- a long growing season, a short mild winter, and the constant presence of water in a landscape that existed at the boundary of land and swamp.
primary_economy: Sugar cane on bayou levees (1800-1960); prairie cattle (1760-1900); rice on converted prairies (1880-1960); fishing, trapping, and moss gathering in swamps and marshes (1760-1960); oil and gas (1901-1960)

calendar_eras.era_name: Agricultural Era -- Sugar, Cattle, and Subsistence
calendar_eras.description: The agricultural calendar in Acadiana was driven by sugar cane in the bayou parishes and cattle on the prairies. Sugar cane imposed the most rigid calendar -- planting in late summer, cultivation through fall and winter growth, and the grinding season from October through January that consumed every available hand. Prairie cattle operations followed a looser rhythm -- spring branding, summer grazing, fall roundup. Subsistence crops (corn, sweet potatoes, vegetables) followed a standard Southern growing calendar. The swamp economy (fishing, trapping, moss gathering) had its own seasons, tied to water levels and animal cycles. The Catholic liturgical calendar overlaid everything -- feast days, fast days, and Sunday Mass structured the week regardless of the crop.

january.agricultural: Sugar grinding season ending (late January). Cane stubble fields dormant. Prairie cattle in winter pasture. Trapping season at peak in marshes and swamps -- muskrat, otter, mink. Hog killing (boucherie) if not done in December.
january.social_civic: Epiphany (January 6) and the beginning of the Mardi Gras season. King cake season begins. Church services. Court sessions at parish seats.
january.economic: Sugar sales from the grinding season providing the year's income for bayou parishes. Trapping pelts being bundled for sale. Credit cycle beginning for the new crop year.
january.weather_conditions: Cool and damp -- 42-55F average. Occasional hard freeze but rarely sustained cold. Gray skies, drizzle, and fog along the bayous. The mildness of Louisiana winters allowed outdoor work year-round.
january.narrative_hook: January in {county} Parish was the end of the grinding season and the beginning of Mardi Gras -- {ancestor_name} had barely washed the sugar from {pronoun_possessive} hands before the king cake appeared on the table.

february.agricultural: Sugar cane land preparation beginning. Stubble being plowed under. Prairie cattle calving. Garden preparation. Trapping season winding down as pelts thin.
february.social_civic: Mardi Gras season at peak. The courir de Mardi Gras -- the Cajun Mardi Gras run where masked horsemen ride from farm to farm collecting ingredients for a communal gumbo. Fat Tuesday celebrations. Lent begins (Ash Wednesday). The transition from carnival excess to Lenten penance was the sharpest cultural boundary in the Cajun calendar.
february.economic: Pre-planting expenses for sugar and subsistence crops. Trapping revenue from pelts.
february.weather_conditions: Cool and variable -- 45-58F. Rain common. Bayous beginning to rise with spring rains.
february.narrative_hook: Mardi Gras was the hinge of {ancestor_name}'s year -- the courir through the countryside, the masked riders demanding chickens and rice for the gumbo, and then Ash Wednesday when the priest put a cross of ashes on every forehead and the feasting stopped.

march.agricultural: Sugar cane planting begins -- seed cane laid in furrows and covered. Corn planted. Sweet potato slips prepared. Prairie cattle on spring grass. Crawfish running in flooded fields and ditches.
march.social_civic: Lent -- abstinence from meat on Fridays, reduced social activity. Easter preparation. Crawfish boils begin (seafood permitted during Lent).
march.economic: Planting expenses at peak. Credit advanced by merchants against the coming crop.
march.weather_conditions: Warming -- 55-68F. Spring rains heavy. Bayous at or near flood stage.
march.narrative_hook: March was Lent and planting -- {ancestor_name} laid seed cane in the furrow by day and ate crawfish on Friday because the church said no meat and the ditches were full of crawfish.

april.agricultural: Sugar cane sprouting. Corn growing. Garden vegetables planted -- tomatoes, peppers, okra, beans. Cattle on full spring pasture.
april.social_civic: Easter -- the most important Catholic feast after Christmas. Easter Mass, family gatherings, special meals. The social season resumed after Lenten restrictions.
april.economic: No crop income yet. Credit accumulating at the store.
april.weather_conditions: Warm and humid -- 62-76F. Thunderstorms frequent. Mosquito season beginning.
april.narrative_hook: Easter in {county} Parish was when the Catholic year turned -- {ancestor_name} at Mass in the morning, at the family table at noon, and by evening the cane was growing and the credit was running.

may.agricultural: Sugar cane cultivation -- hoeing and plowing between rows. Corn tasseling. Garden production beginning. Cattle on summer range.
may.social_civic: Church services. Community gatherings limited by agricultural work. May processions at Catholic churches (honoring the Virgin Mary).
may.economic: No income. Credit deepening. Lean season.
may.weather_conditions: Hot and humid -- 70-84F. Daily thunderstorms. Mosquitoes intense.
may.narrative_hook: May was when the heat arrived and did not leave until October -- {ancestor_name} worked the cane rows in air so thick that sweat was pointless, and the mosquitoes were a second crop that never failed.

june.agricultural: Sugar cane growing vigorously. Second cultivation. Shrimping season beginning in coastal parishes. Prairie hay cutting.
june.social_civic: Corpus Christi feast day. Church fairs (bazaars) -- the primary fundraising and social events of the Catholic parishes.
june.economic: No crop income. Shrimp providing coastal income. Store credit at maximum.
june.weather_conditions: Hot and very humid -- 76-90F. Hurricane season beginning (June 1).
june.narrative_hook: June was when the cane grew faster than a man could hoe it -- {ancestor_name} fought the grass in the rows and trusted God and the weather with the rest.

july.agricultural: Sugar cane lay-by (less cultivation needed as cane canopy closes). Rice transplanting/flooding in prairie parishes after 1880. Shrimping at peak.
july.social_civic: July 4th observed modestly. Fais do-do dances on Saturday nights. Bastille Day (July 14) sometimes acknowledged in French communities. Church feast days.
july.economic: Lean season. Some families supplemented by fishing and moss gathering.
july.weather_conditions: Hottest month -- 78-92F. Humidity near 100%. Afternoon thunderstorms daily. Hurricane risk.
july.narrative_hook: July in {county} Parish was the long waiting -- the cane growing on its own, the rice flooding in the paddies, and {ancestor_name} at the fais do-do on Saturday night dancing in air that felt like warm water.

august.agricultural: Sugar cane maturing. Rice heading. Shrimping peak. Garden production at maximum -- okra, tomatoes, peppers for preserving.
august.social_civic: Feast of the Assumption (August 15) -- major Catholic holy day. Church bazaars and family gatherings.
august.economic: Anticipation of grinding season income. Shrimp sales providing coastal income.
august.weather_conditions: Hot and humid -- 78-92F. Peak hurricane season (August-September). The 1856 hurricane that destroyed Last Island killed over 100 people from Acadiana families.
august.narrative_hook: The Feast of the Assumption in August was when {ancestor_name}'s parish gathered -- Mass in the morning, the bazaar in the afternoon, and the knowledge that the grinding season was six weeks away.

september.agricultural: Sugar cane harvest preparation. Rice harvest beginning in prairie parishes. Fall cattle roundup on prairies. Trapping preparation.
september.social_civic: Limited social activity as harvest demands intensified.
september.economic: Rice income beginning in prairie parishes. Sugar income imminent.
september.weather_conditions: Still hot -- 74-88F. Hurricane risk at peak. The hurricane of September 1893 devastated Cheniere Caminada and coastal Acadiana.
september.narrative_hook: September was when {ancestor_name} watched the sky -- the cane was ready but so were the hurricanes, and a single storm could destroy a year's work in an afternoon.

october.agricultural: SUGAR GRINDING SEASON BEGINS -- the defining event of the bayou economy. Cane cut by hand (machete), hauled to sugar house, crushed, boiled, crystallized. Grinding ran day and night for 3-4 months. Every available hand worked. Rice harvest at peak in prairie parishes.
october.social_civic: All Souls' Day preparation (November 1-2). Social life suspended by grinding season demands.
october.economic: Sugar income beginning. Rice income at peak. The year's economic fate determined.
october.weather_conditions: Cooling -- 62-78F. First cool fronts. The most pleasant month weather-wise -- cool enough to work the sugar house without heat exhaustion.
october.narrative_hook: When the grinding started in October, {ancestor_name}'s world contracted to the sugar house and the cane field -- twelve-hour shifts, the smell of boiling juice, and the knowledge that every hogshead was money against the store account.

november.agricultural: Grinding season at full intensity. Rice harvest completed. Fall planting of winter vegetables. Trapping season opening in marshes.
november.social_civic: All Saints' Day and All Souls' Day (November 1-2) -- cemetery cleaning, decoration, and prayer for the dead. Toussaint (All Saints') was a major community event. Thanksgiving observed modestly (secondary to Catholic calendar).
november.economic: Sugar sales beginning. Trapping income starting.
november.weather_conditions: Cool and pleasant -- 50-66F. First frost in northern parishes (Evangeline, St. Landry) by late November.
november.narrative_hook: On All Saints' Day, {ancestor_name} cleaned the family tombs at the parish cemetery -- whitewashing the above-ground vaults, placing flowers, and praying for the dead in a tradition that connected the living to every generation before them.

december.agricultural: Grinding season continuing (through January). Hog killing (boucherie) in the piney woods and prairie parishes. Trapping at peak.
december.social_civic: Christmas -- the paramount Catholic feast. Midnight Mass (Messe de Minuit) on Christmas Eve. Reveillon supper after Mass -- a multi-course feast with gumbo, boudin, roast pork, and rice dressing. Visiting among families through the Christmas week. Preparations for Mardi Gras season beginning.
december.economic: Sugar income at peak. Trapping income beginning to flow. Year-end debt settlement with merchants.
december.weather_conditions: Cool -- 42-58F. Occasional frost. Rarely cold enough for sustained hard freeze in the bayou parishes.
december.narrative_hook: Christmas Eve Midnight Mass at {church_name} was {ancestor_name}'s anchor -- the Latin liturgy, the French hymns, and the Reveillon supper afterward where the gumbo was on the table before the priest had unlaced his vestments.

calendar_eras.era_name: Mixed Economy Era -- Sugar, Rice, Oil, and Fishing
calendar_eras.description: After 1900, the Acadiana calendar was no longer driven solely by agriculture. The oil industry operated year-round with no seasonal variation -- drilling rigs did not observe harvest seasons. Rice had its own calendar (spring planting, fall harvest) that overlapped but did not synchronize with sugar. Commercial shrimping and fishing followed marine cycles. The result was a more complex calendar where different families in the same parish lived on different seasonal rhythms depending on their primary income: the sugar family watched the cane, the rice family watched the water, the oil family watched the drilling schedule, and the shrimper watched the tides.

january.agricultural: Sugar grinding season ending. Rice fields dormant. Oil drilling year-round. Trapping at peak.
january.social_civic: Epiphany and king cake season. Mardi Gras preparation begins. Oil industry brought its own social calendar -- company parties, service club meetings in Lafayette.
january.economic: Sugar sales concluding. Trapping income. Oil wages year-round.
january.narrative_hook: In January {year}, {ancestor_name} was either finishing the grinding, checking trap lines, or punching a time clock at the drilling company -- Acadiana's calendar had split into three economies that shared a parish but not a rhythm.

february.agricultural: Land preparation for sugar and rice. Oil drilling continuous.
february.social_civic: Mardi Gras at peak. The courir de Mardi Gras continued in rural communities. Lafayette's urban Mardi Gras grew into a regional celebration after 1934.
february.narrative_hook: Mardi Gras in {year} was when {ancestor_name}'s Acadiana showed its best face -- the courir through the countryside, the masked riders in their screen-wire masks demanding chickens for the gumbo, and the whole community dancing before the Lenten fast.

march.agricultural: Sugar planting. Rice fields being prepared and flooded. Oil drilling continuous. Crawfish season.
march.social_civic: Lent. Easter preparation. Crawfish boils (permitted during Lent).
march.economic: Planting credit extended. Oil wages steady.
march.narrative_hook: March was when {ancestor_name} planted cane or flooded rice paddies while the oil rigs drilled through Lent without stopping -- the oil economy did not observe the Catholic calendar.

april.agricultural: Sugar sprouting. Rice growing in flooded paddies. Shrimping season beginning offshore.
april.social_civic: Easter. Parish bazaars. Oil company picnics.
april.economic: No crop income. Oil wages steady. Shrimp sales beginning.
april.narrative_hook: Easter Sunday in {county} Parish was the one day when {ancestor_name} could be certain that every person at Mass would be there -- farmer, oilman, shrimper, and store clerk, all in the same pew.

may.agricultural: Sugar and rice cultivation. Offshore shrimping expanding. Oil drilling continuous.
may.social_civic: May processions. Growing season social lull.
may.economic: Lean season for agriculture. Oil and shrimping income steady.
may.narrative_hook: By May the heat had settled in, and {ancestor_name} worked whatever the economy demanded -- cane rows, rice levees, shrimp nets, or pipe wrenches.

june.agricultural: Sugar growing. Rice flooding at peak. Brown shrimp season. Oil drilling continuous.
june.economic: Shrimp sales. Oil wages. No crop income.
june.weather_conditions: Hot and humid -- 76-90F. Hurricane season begins.
june.narrative_hook: June in the coastal parishes meant shrimp and hurricane worry in equal measure -- {ancestor_name} worked the nets and watched the barometer.

july.agricultural: Sugar lay-by. Rice maturing. White shrimp season beginning. Oil drilling continuous.
july.economic: Shrimp income. Oil wages. Agricultural lean season.
july.narrative_hook: The fais do-do on Saturday night was still {ancestor_name}'s anchor -- the accordion, the French lyrics, and the two-step on a plank floor that the oil company had not yet paved over.

august.agricultural: Sugar maturing. Rice heading. Peak shrimping. Oil continuous.
august.social_civic: Assumption Day. Shrimp festivals in coastal towns.
august.economic: Strong shrimp income. Oil wages. Harvest anticipation.
august.narrative_hook: The shrimp boats blessed by the priest at the Blessing of the Fleet in August were {ancestor_name}'s livelihood and {pronoun_possessive} prayer -- the church and the Gulf meeting at the dock.

september.agricultural: Sugar harvest preparation. Rice harvest beginning. Shrimping continuing. Oil continuous.
september.economic: Rice income beginning. Sugar income imminent.
september.narrative_hook: September was the month {ancestor_name} watched the Gulf for hurricanes and the cane for maturity -- both could make or break the year in a single day.

october.agricultural: GRINDING SEASON BEGINS. Rice harvest at peak. Shrimping continuing. Oil continuous.
october.social_civic: All Souls' preparation. Grinding season dominates bayou social calendar.
october.economic: Peak income month. Sugar, rice, shrimp all producing revenue simultaneously.
october.narrative_hook: October was payday for all of Acadiana at once -- sugar grinding, rice threshing, shrimp landing, and the oil check on Friday. {ancestor_name} worked whichever one paid the bills.

november.agricultural: Grinding at full intensity. Rice harvest completed. Trapping season opens. Oil continuous.
november.social_civic: All Saints' Day -- cemetery cleaning and decoration. Toussaint observances.
november.economic: Strong income month across all sectors.
november.narrative_hook: On All Saints' Day, {ancestor_name} whitewashed the family vault at the cemetery and placed chrysanthemums in the iron holders -- the dead were not forgotten in Acadiana, and the above-ground tombs kept them visible.

december.agricultural: Grinding continuing. Trapping at peak. Oil continuous.
december.social_civic: Christmas. Midnight Mass. Reveillon supper. Preparations for Mardi Gras.
december.economic: Year-end. Debt settlement. Income from sugar, trapping, and oil.
december.narrative_hook: The Reveillon supper after Midnight Mass was {ancestor_name}'s Christmas -- gumbo, boudin, rice dressing, and black coffee at 2 AM, the family gathered in a tradition that had survived the Grand Derangement and two centuries of Louisiana winters.

narrative_hooks: The grinding season was {ancestor_name}'s world from October to January -- the sugar house ran day and night, the air smelled of boiling cane juice, and every hogshead was money against the store account.
Mardi Gras was the hinge -- {ancestor_name} danced and feasted until Ash Wednesday, and then the church said stop and the fields said start, and both were obeyed.
The Reveillon supper after Midnight Mass was Christmas in Acadiana -- gumbo at 2 AM, the family gathered, and French spoken at a table that had been set this way since the exile.
All Saints' Day was when {ancestor_name} tended the dead -- whitewashing the above-ground tombs, placing flowers, and remembering names that traced back to Nova Scotia.
The oil economy did not observe the Catholic calendar -- the rigs drilled through Lent, through Christmas, through the grinding season, and {ancestor_name} punched the clock regardless.
The crawfish boil in March was Lent's gift to Acadiana -- no meat on Friday, but the crawfish in the ditches were not meat, and {ancestor_name}'s table was red with cayenne and shells.
source_references: Post, Lauren C. Cajun Sketches. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 1962.
Sitterson, J. Carlyle. Sugar Country. Lexington: University of Kentucky Press, 1953.
Bernard, Shane K. The Cajuns. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 2003.
Ancelet, Barry Jean, Jay Edwards, and Glen Pitre. Cajun Country. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 1991.
Brasseaux, Carl A. Acadian to Cajun. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 1992.
Gutierrez, C. Paige. Cajun Foodways. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 1992.
